title: Paying for Rehabilitation Care: What to Expect
summary: Rehab costs and coverage rules can be confusing, especially around how long insurance will pay for a stay. Here's the general shape of it.
category: Life Transitions I: The Hospital-to-Home Arc
journeyStage: Life Transitions I: The Hospital-to-Home Arc
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Paying for Rehabilitation Care: What to Expect | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Rehab costs and coverage rules can be confusing, especially around how long insurance will pay for a stay. Here's the general shape of it.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Medicare typically covers a limited number of days for skilled rehab, with specific qualifying conditions.
Coverage can end based on "progress," not just a fixed calendar date.
A facility's billing office or social worker can clarify exact numbers for your situation.

paragraphs: Medicare's coverage for skilled rehab generally requires a prior qualifying hospital stay and has a defined benefit period, with cost-sharing that increases the longer the stay continues — but the exact details depend on the specific plan and situation, so general summaries only go so far.

Importantly, coverage isn't just about hitting a day count — it can end earlier if the facility determines a patient isn't continuing to show functional progress, which is why understanding the stated goals of the stay matters for the family too.

nextSteps: Ask the facility's social worker for the specific coverage timeline early in the stay.
Understand what "progress" benchmarks are being used.
Ask about supplemental insurance or Medicaid options if a longer stay seems likely.
disclaimer: General information, not financial advice — a facility's billing office or a Medicare/Medicaid counselor can address your specific coverage details.

sources.name: Medicare.gov: Medicare Coverage of Skilled Nursing Facility Care
